## Maternal Deaths Declining But Haemorrhage Remains the Leading Killer at 35% — Review Coverage Still Below 75% [WARNING]

### Mortality Trend (8-Quarter Series)

| Quarter | Maternal Deaths | Stillbirths | Early Neonatal | MDR Review % |
|---------|----------------|-------------|---------------|-------------|
| Q1 2023 | 28 | 142 | 98 | 45% |
| Q2 2023 | 32 | 155 | 105 | 52% |
| Q3 2023 | 35 | 168 | 112 | 48% |
| Q4 2023 | 30 | 148 | 95 | 55% |
| Q1 2024 | 27 | 138 | 88 | 62% |
| Q2 2024 | 25 | 132 | 82 | 68% |
| Q3 2024 | 29 | 145 | 92 | 65% |
| Q4 2024 | 22 | 125 | 78 | 72% |
| **Change** | **-21.4%** | **-12.0%** | **-20.4%** | **+27 pts** |

### Cause of Maternal Death

| Cause | Share | Preventability | Key Intervention |
|-------|-------|---------------|------------------|
| Haemorrhage | 35% [CRITICAL] | Highly preventable | Active management of 3rd stage, oxytocin, blood |
| Hypertensive disorders | 22% [CRITICAL] | Preventable with monitoring | ANC BP screening, MgSO4, timely delivery |
| Sepsis | 18% [WARNING] | Preventable with IPC | Aseptic technique, prophylactic antibiotics |
| Obstructed labour | 12% [WARNING] | Preventable with EmONC | Partograph use, timely C-section/referral |
| Unsafe abortion | 8% [WARNING] | Preventable with FP/PAC | Contraception access, post-abortion care |
| Other direct | 5% [INFO] | Variable | Case-specific review |

### Trend Analysis

- **[GOOD] Maternal deaths declined from 35 (Q3 2023 peak) to 22 (Q4 2024)**: 37% reduction over 5 quarters — meaningful improvement, likely reflecting EmONC and MPDSR investments
- **[WARNING] Q3 seasonal pattern visible**: Both 2023 and 2024 show Q3 spikes (35 and 29 respectively) — correlates with rainy season, reduced facility access, and malaria-in-pregnancy peaks
- **[GOOD] Early neonatal deaths declined 20.4%**: Parallel to maternal death reduction — confirming that intrapartum care improvements benefit both mothers and newborns
- **[WARNING] Stillbirths declined only 12.0% vs 21.4% for maternal deaths**: Stillbirth reduction is lagging — many stillbirths occur before arrival at facility (macerated stillbirths) and are less amenable to facility-based interventions

### MPDSR Review Coverage

- **[WARNING] MDR review at 72% (Q4 2024)**: Improved from 45% but still below the 100% target. 28% of maternal deaths are NOT reviewed — the very cases that could reveal preventable system failures
- The improving trend (45% to 72%) suggests institutional commitment is growing, but the Q3 2024 dip (65%) indicates review capacity is vulnerable to competing priorities
- Deaths not reviewed are disproportionately likely to be community deaths, home deaths, or deaths at lower-level facilities — precisely where lessons are most needed

### Cross-Program Intelligence

- Haemorrhage at 35% links directly to EmONC signal function gaps — facilities without oxytocin, blood transfusion, or surgical capacity cannot manage PPH
- Hypertensive disorders at 22% link to ANC quality — BP monitoring and proteinuria screening at every ANC visit are the detection mechanism
- Unsafe abortion at 8% links to family planning coverage — every unmet FP need is a potential unsafe abortion. The FP-mortality causal chain is direct
- Sepsis at 18% links to IPC and WASH — verify facility-level IPC scores and water availability in labour wards

### Data Quality Assessment

- Maternal death counts depend on notification completeness — community deaths and early pregnancy deaths may be underreported
- The Q3 2023 spike (35 deaths) could partly reflect improved notification rather than true increase — cross-reference with facility delivery volumes
- Stillbirth classification (fresh vs macerated) would distinguish intrapartum care quality issues from pre-arrival deaths
- Cause-of-death assignment requires verbal autopsy or medical certification — verify the attribution methodology

### Recommendations

1. **Immediate — Target haemorrhage interventions**: Active management of third stage of labour (AMTSL) with oxytocin should be 100% at every delivery. Verify oxytocin stock and cold chain at all delivery points
2. **Short-term — Achieve 100% MDR review**: Assign dedicated MPDSR focal persons at district level. Every unreviewed death is a missed learning opportunity. Prioritize reviewing community and lower-facility deaths
3. **Short-term — Seasonal preparedness for Q3**: Pre-position emergency commodities, ensure referral vehicles are functional, and strengthen malaria-in-pregnancy prevention before the rainy season peak
4. **Medium-term — Stillbirth reduction strategy**: Fresh stillbirth rates (intrapartum) should be tracked separately — these are preventable with partograph monitoring and timely intervention. Macerated stillbirths require antenatal interventions
5. **Medium-term — Close the unsafe abortion gap**: Strengthen post-abortion care availability and expand FP access — the 8% contribution to maternal death is entirely preventable with rights-based contraception services

## Karamoja in Nutrition Emergency — 55% Stunting and 15% Wasting Far Exceed Crisis Thresholds [CRITICAL]

| District | GMP Coverage | Underweight | Stunting | Wasting | Excl. BF | Overall Status |
|----------|-------------|-------------|----------|---------|----------|----------------|
| Kampala | 72% | 8% | 22% | 3% | 65% | [WARNING] Moderate |
| Wakiso | 65% | 10% | 25% | 4% | 58% | [WARNING] Moderate |
| Lira | 48% | 14% | 35% | 6% | 48% | [WARNING] High |
| Gulu | 42% | 16% | 38% | 7% | 45% | [CRITICAL] Very high |
| Soroti | 38% | 18% | 42% | 8% | 40% | [CRITICAL] Very high |
| Karamoja | 22% | 28% | 55% | 15% | 28% | [CRITICAL] Emergency |

### WHO Classification Thresholds Applied

| Indicator | Low | Medium | High | Very High/Emergency | Karamoja |
|-----------|-----|--------|------|--------------------|-----------|
| Stunting | <20% | 20-29% | 30-39% | >=40% | 55% [CRITICAL] |
| Wasting | <5% | 5-9% | 10-14% | >=15% | 15% [CRITICAL] |
| Underweight | <10% | 10-19% | 20-29% | >=30% | 28% [CRITICAL] |

- **[CRITICAL] Karamoja wasting at 15% equals the emergency threshold**: This triggers the need for emergency nutrition response — CMAM scale-up, blanket supplementary feeding, and food security intervention
- **[CRITICAL] Karamoja stunting at 55%**: More than half of children under 2 are chronically malnourished — this represents irreversible developmental damage affecting cognitive potential, educational attainment, and lifetime productivity
- **[CRITICAL] Karamoja GMP coverage at 22%**: The monitoring system itself is failing — 78% of children are not being weighed and assessed. The true malnutrition burden is likely even worse than measured
- **[WARNING] Exclusive breastfeeding at 28% in Karamoja vs 65% in Kampala**: The 37-point gap in the most fundamental infant feeding practice reflects food insecurity, cultural practices, and lack of BF counseling

### Gradient Analysis

- Perfect inverse correlation between GMP coverage and malnutrition indicators — districts that monitor less have worse outcomes (and likely even worse unmeasured outcomes)
- The nutrition gradient mirrors the general health service gradient: Central > Western > Northern > Eastern > Karamoja
- Stunting prevalence doubles from Kampala (22%) to Karamoja (55%) — a single country with first-world and crisis-level nutrition existing simultaneously

### Cross-Program Intelligence

- Stunting at 42-55% predicts poor school readiness and cognitive outcomes — education sector should plan for learning support in these districts
- High wasting correlates with higher case fatality from malaria, pneumonia, and diarrhoea — child mortality in Karamoja and Soroti is amplified by malnutrition
- Low exclusive BF rates increase diarrhoeal disease risk 6-10x — WASH investment alone cannot compensate for poor infant feeding
- Maternal nutrition during pregnancy drives LBW which drives early stunting — maternal BMI and dietary diversity data are needed to complete the picture
- Food insecurity in Karamoja is linked to pastoralist livelihood disruption, climate variability, and conflict — health sector alone cannot solve this

### Data Quality Considerations

- GMP coverage at 22% in Karamoja means nutrition indicators are based on the ~22% who attend services — likely the more accessible, less food-insecure families. True population prevalence is probably worse
- Stunting requires length/height measurement with standardized boards — measurement quality at community level may introduce error
- Exclusive BF rates are typically reported by mothers at facility visits — social desirability bias may inflate reported rates
- Wasting prevalence from routine GMP data differs from survey-based SMART methodology — compare with most recent SMART survey for validation

### Recommendations

1. **Emergency — Karamoja nutrition emergency declaration**: Activate emergency nutrition response including CMAM scale-up, blanket supplementary feeding (BSFP) for all children 6-23 months, and food security coordination with WFP/UNICEF
2. **Immediate — IYCF counseling surge**: Deploy trained infant and young child feeding counselors in all districts below 50% exclusive BF — focus on first 1,000 days messaging, especially in Karamoja and Soroti
3. **Short-term — GMP coverage expansion**: Integrate GMP into all immunization contacts, ANC visits, and community outreach. A child weighed is a child monitored — the 22% coverage in Karamoja leaves 78% invisible
4. **Medium-term — Multi-sectoral nutrition strategy**: Stunting at 42-55% cannot be solved by health sector alone — coordinate with agriculture (food security), WASH (infection reduction), education (school feeding), and social protection (cash transfers)
5. **Data action — Validate with SMART survey**: Commission a population-based SMART nutrition survey in Karamoja and Soroti to establish true prevalence independent of facility-based data biases

## Karamoja TB Treatment Success at 55% — Far Below WHO 90% Target With 28% Loss to Follow-Up [CRITICAL]

| District | CNR/100K | TSR | LTFU | Death | Bact. Conf. | HIV Test | Status |
|----------|---------|-----|------|-------|------------|----------|--------|
| Kampala | 185 | 78% | 12% | 5% | 72% | 95% | [WARNING] |
| Wakiso | 145 | 82% | 8% | 4% | 68% | 92% | [WARNING] |
| Gulu | 92 | 75% | 15% | 6% | 48% | 82% | [WARNING] |
| Lira | 88 | 72% | 16% | 7% | 45% | 78% | [CRITICAL] |
| Soroti | 75 | 68% | 18% | 8% | 38% | 72% | [CRITICAL] |
| Karamoja | 42 | 55% | 28% | 12% | 22% | 58% | [CRITICAL] |
| **WHO target** | -- | **>90%** | **<5%** | **<5%** | **>80%** | **100%** | -- |

### Case Finding Analysis

- **[CRITICAL] Karamoja CNR at 42/100K is suspiciously low**: Given high HIV prevalence and malnutrition (both TB risk factors), the true incidence is likely much higher. This represents massive underdetection, not low disease burden
- **[WARNING] Kampala CNR at 185/100K is the highest but may reflect better detection, not higher incidence**: Urban facilities with GeneXpert access find more cases — the notification gap between Kampala (185) and Karamoja (42) is 4.4x
- **[WARNING] Bacteriological confirmation drops from 72% (Kampala) to 22% (Karamoja)**: This directly reflects GeneXpert/microscopy access. Clinical-only TB diagnosis at 78% in Karamoja means high rates of misdiagnosis — both over-diagnosis (treating non-TB) and under-diagnosis (missing TB)

### Treatment Outcomes

- **[CRITICAL] No district meets the WHO 90% treatment success target**: Even the best performer (Wakiso, 82%) is 8 points below — this is a national TB program crisis
- **[CRITICAL] Karamoja LTFU at 28%**: More than 1 in 4 TB patients stops treatment — this generates drug resistance, continued community transmission, and individual mortality
- **[CRITICAL] Karamoja death rate at 12%**: 2.4x the WHO target. High death rates in TB are typically driven by: late diagnosis, HIV coinfection without ART, malnutrition, and poor access to inpatient care
- **[WARNING] Kampala LTFU at 12% despite urban access**: Urban LTFU is driven by mobility, informal settlement conditions, and alcohol/substance use — different determinants than rural LTFU

### HIV-TB Integration

- **[WARNING] HIV testing among TB patients at 58% in Karamoja vs 95% in Kampala**: The 37-point gap means 42% of TB patients in Karamoja have unknown HIV status — TB-HIV coinfection is the leading cause of TB death, and untested patients cannot receive life-saving ART
- The correlation between HIV testing rates and treatment success is strong — districts with higher HIV testing have better TSR, likely because co-infected patients receive integrated ART+TB treatment

### Cross-Program Links

- TB-malnutrition: Karamoja's 55% stunting rate (from nutrition data) directly predisposes to TB. Nutritional support should be part of every TB treatment plan in this district
- TB-HIV: The 58% HIV testing rate means the collaborative TB/HIV program is failing in Karamoja — every TB patient must be offered HIV testing and vice versa
- TB case finding should be integrated with HIV care (intensified TB case finding at ART clinics) and with community outreach
- Drug-resistant TB risk: 28% LTFU generates MDR-TB — DST (drug susceptibility testing) is critical in this context

### Data Quality Assessment

- Low CNR may indicate underreporting rather than low burden — compare with active case-finding survey data if available
- Bacteriological confirmation below 50% makes treatment outcomes unreliable — some "TB deaths" may be non-TB patients who were empirically treated
- Cohort outcomes should sum to ~100% (TSR + LTFU + death + failure + not evaluated). Verify completeness
- Karamoja data may have significant reporting delays given access challenges

### Recommendations

1. **Emergency — GeneXpert expansion to Karamoja**: 22% bacteriological confirmation is unacceptable. Deploy GeneXpert with solar power solutions and train lab technicians. Accurate diagnosis is the foundation of everything else
2. **Immediate — TB-HIV integration in Karamoja/Soroti**: Implement provider-initiated testing and counseling (PITC) for all TB patients. 42% untested is 42% potentially dying from untreated HIV
3. **Short-term — Community-based DOT**: Replace facility-based DOT with community DOT supporters in high-LTFU districts. 28% LTFU cannot be solved by telling patients to come to the clinic
4. **Short-term — Nutritional support for TB patients**: Provide food packages or cash transfers during TB treatment — malnourished patients have worse outcomes and higher default rates
5. **Medium-term — Active case finding campaigns**: Conduct community-based TB screening in Karamoja using mobile X-ray and GeneXpert — the 42/100K notification rate hides a much larger epidemic

## HepB Birth Dose Below 20% in 4 Districts — Vertical Transmission Prevention Failing Despite High Maternal Prevalence [CRITICAL]

| District | ANC HBsAg Screen | Positive Rate | Birth Dose | HepB3 Infant | Status |
|----------|-----------------|---------------|------------|-------------|--------|
| Kampala | 68% | 4.2% | 52% | 85% | [WARNING] |
| Wakiso | 55% | 3.8% | 42% | 82% | [WARNING] |
| Lira | 32% | 5.1% | 22% | 75% | [CRITICAL] |
| Gulu | 28% | 5.5% | 18% | 72% | [CRITICAL] |
| Soroti | 22% | 6.2% | 12% | 68% | [CRITICAL] |
| Karamoja | 8% | 8.5% | 5% | 45% | [CRITICAL] |
| **WHO target** | **>90%** | -- | **>80%** | **>90%** | -- |

### Prevention-of-Mother-to-Child-Transmission (HBV-PMTCT) Cascade Failure

- **[CRITICAL] Karamoja HepB birth dose at 5%**: 95% of newborns born to HBsAg-positive mothers receive no birth dose — WHO recommends birth dose within 24 hours to prevent vertical transmission, which causes 90% chronic infection in neonates
- **[CRITICAL] Inverse relationship between prevalence and screening**: Karamoja has the highest positivity (8.5%) but lowest screening (8%) — the highest-risk population is the least screened. This is the definition of an inverted care law
- **[WARNING] Even Kampala birth dose at only 52%**: The best-performing district still misses half of newborns — birth dose requires vaccine available in the delivery room 24/7, which is a cold chain and logistics challenge
- **[INFO] HepB3 infant series outperforms birth dose everywhere**: HepB3 at 45-85% vs birth dose at 5-52% — the EPI schedule at 6/10/14 weeks is working through routine immunization, but the critical birth dose is being missed

### Epidemiological Significance

- HBsAg prevalence of 3.8-8.5% classifies this as a high-endemicity setting (>8% in Karamoja = hyperendemic)
- Each unprotected neonate born to HBsAg+ mother has a 70-90% chance of chronic HBV infection, leading to lifetime risk of cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma
- At Karamoja's 8.5% prevalence with 5% birth dose coverage, an estimated 95% of neonatal HBV infections are not being prevented

### Cross-Program Integration Opportunities

- **ANC-HBV integration**: HBsAg screening should piggyback on HIV testing at ANC (already 58-95% coverage) — dual testing kits exist
- **EPI-birth dose logistics**: Birth dose requires vaccine at every delivery point with cold chain — same infrastructure challenge as oxytocin cold chain for maternal health
- **HIV-HBV coinfection**: Tenofovir-based ART regimens cover both HIV and HBV — HBV screening in PMTCT programs identifies co-infected mothers needing treatment for both
- **Liver cancer prevention**: HBV elimination is cancer prevention — link to NCD program for long-term chronic HBV management

### Data Quality Notes

- Low screening rates (8-28%) mean positivity rates may be biased — screened women may be systematically different from unscreened women
- Birth dose coverage depends on accurate recording of vaccination within 24 hours — doses given after 24 hours are less effective but may still be counted
- Karamoja's 8.5% prevalence from only 8% screened is imprecise — true population prevalence requires serosurvey data

### Recommendations

1. **Immediate — Integrate HBsAg rapid test into ANC HIV testing**: Add HBsAg rapid test at first ANC visit alongside HIV test — uses same finger-prick, same counseling flow, minimal additional cost
2. **Immediate — Birth dose at every delivery point**: Procure monovalent HepB vaccine in birth dose presentation, ensure cold chain at all delivery facilities, and train midwives to administer within 24 hours
3. **Short-term — Hepatitis B treatment for HBsAg+ pregnant women**: WHO recommends tenofovir prophylaxis for high-viral-load mothers — integrate into ANC protocol alongside PMTCT
4. **Medium-term — HBV elimination roadmap**: Achieving WHO's 2030 elimination targets requires >90% birth dose and >90% HepB3 — current trajectory makes this impossible without a surge strategy

## Young Women 15-24 Bear Highest STI Burden — Partner Notification Below 35% Perpetuates Reinfection Cycle [CRITICAL]

| Age Group | Female Cases | Male Cases | F:M Ratio | Adequate Tx (F/M) | Partner Notif (F/M) |
|-----------|-------------|------------|-----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 15-19 | 1,850 | 680 | 2.7:1 | 62% / 58% | 28% / 22% |
| 20-24 | 3,200 | 1,800 | 1.8:1 | 68% / 65% | 32% / 30% |
| 25-29 | 2,800 | 2,200 | 1.3:1 | 72% / 70% | 38% / 35% |
| 30-39 | 2,100 | 2,500 | 0.8:1 | 75% / 72% | 42% / 40% |
| 40-49 | 800 | 1,200 | 0.7:1 | 78% / 75% | 48% / 45% |
| **Total** | **10,750** | **8,380** | **1.3:1** | -- | -- |

### Age-Sex Pyramid Interpretation

- **[CRITICAL] 15-19 female-to-male ratio of 2.7:1**: Young women present with STIs at nearly 3x the rate of young men. This is driven by biological susceptibility (cervical ectopy in adolescents), age-disparate sexual partnerships, and better health-seeking behavior among females
- **[CRITICAL] 15-19 adequate treatment at only 58-62%**: The youngest patients receive the worst care — nearly 40% of adolescent STI cases are inadequately treated. This drives antimicrobial resistance and complications (PID, infertility, ectopic pregnancy)
- **[CRITICAL] Partner notification below 30% in under-25s**: Without partner treatment, 70%+ of patients will be reinfected — syndromic management without partner management is a revolving door
- **[INFO] Treatment adequacy improves with age (58-78%)**: Older patients at higher-level facilities with more experienced providers likely drive this gradient

### Epidemiological Patterns

- The pyramid is bottom-heavy for females (peak at 20-24) and more evenly distributed for males — consistent with age-disparate sexual networks
- The F:M ratio inverts after age 30 (0.8:1 at 30-39, 0.7:1 at 40-49) — men present later, possibly when symptomatic with urethral discharge, while women's STIs are more often asymptomatic and detected through screening
- Total of 19,130 STI episodes in one quarter is substantial — annualized to ~76,000, this represents significant reproductive health morbidity

### Cross-Program Links

- **STI-HIV synergy**: Untreated STIs increase HIV acquisition risk 2-5x. The 28% partner notification in 15-19 year-olds directly undermines HIV prevention
- **STI-FP integration**: STI screening should be routine at FP clinics, especially for young women accessing injectable contraceptives (frequent contacts, no barrier protection)
- **STI-ANC link**: Syphilis and chlamydia in pregnancy cause stillbirth, prematurity, and neonatal infection — link to ANC screening data
- **STI-adolescent SRH**: The 15-19 burden connects to comprehensive sexuality education and adolescent-friendly services
- **AMR risk**: Low treatment adequacy (38-42% receiving incorrect regimens) accelerates gonococcal AMR — syndromic management guidelines may need updating to reflect local resistance patterns

### Data Quality Assessment

- Syndromic management data captures treated cases but misses asymptomatic infections (particularly chlamydia in women) — true burden is likely 2-3x higher
- Male underrepresentation is expected but may also reflect stigma-related avoidance of public facilities — men may use pharmacies or private clinics not captured in HMIS
- "Adequately treated" definition should be verified — does it mean correct drug, correct dose, and correct duration per national STI guidelines?
- Partner notification rates may be inflated if self-reported rather than verified

### Recommendations

1. **Immediate — Update STI treatment protocols**: If 38-42% are inadequately treated, verify that providers have current national guidelines and correct antibiotics in stock. Consider pre-packaged STI treatment kits
2. **Immediate — Strengthen partner notification**: Implement assisted partner notification (provider gives patient contact cards for partners) — shown to increase notification rates 2-3x over passive notification
3. **Short-term — Integrate STI screening into FP and ANC**: Every FP visit for young women and every ANC visit should include STI syndromic assessment — these are high-volume contact points
4. **Short-term — Youth-friendly STI services**: Reduce stigma barriers by offering confidential, non-judgmental STI services during extended hours, with male-friendly entry points
5. **Medium-term — Transition from purely syndromic to etiological management**: Where GeneXpert CT/NG is available, point-of-care STI testing improves treatment accuracy and generates AMR surveillance data

## Cholera Outbreak Peaking at W50 — Lira CTC Has 1 Day IV Fluid Supply With 95% Bed Occupancy [CRITICAL]

### Epidemic Curve Analysis

| Week | Confirmed | Suspected | Total | Trend | Confirmation Rate |
|------|-----------|-----------|-------|-------|-------------------|
| W48 | 42 | 128 | 170 | Baseline | 24.7% |
| W49 | 85 | 245 | 330 | +94% | 25.8% |
| W50 | 168 | 412 | 580 | +76% peak | 29.0% |
| W51 | 125 | 298 | 423 | -27% | 29.6% |
| W52 | 78 | 185 | 263 | -38% | 29.7% |
| **Total** | **498** | **1,268** | **1,766** | -- | **28.2%** |

- **[GOOD] Epidemic curve shows descending limb**: Cases peaked at W50 (580) and declined to 263 by W52 — suggesting response measures are taking effect
- **[WARNING] Declining case counts do not mean outbreak is over**: Cholera outbreaks commonly show multiple waves. Premature scaling down of response has caused resurgences in similar outbreaks
- **[INFO] Confirmation rate steady at 25-30%**: Consistent rate suggests testing capacity is keeping pace. The 70% suspected-only cases should receive treatment regardless but culture confirmation should continue for AMR surveillance

### Case Fatality by Age

| Age Group | CFR | WHO Benchmark (<1%) | Status |
|-----------|-----|--------------------|---------|
| 0-4 years | 4.8% | >1% | [CRITICAL] |
| 5-14 years | 1.2% | >1% | [WARNING] |
| 15-49 years | 0.8% | <1% | [GOOD] |
| 50+ years | 3.5% | >1% | [CRITICAL] |

- **[CRITICAL] Under-5 CFR at 4.8% is a clinical emergency**: Nearly 5x the WHO target. Young children dehydrate faster and require immediate aggressive IV rehydration. This CFR suggests late presentation or inadequate pediatric fluid management
- **[CRITICAL] Over-50 CFR at 3.5%**: Elderly patients with comorbidities (diabetes, renal disease, cardiovascular) are at elevated risk. May also reflect delayed care-seeking
- **[GOOD] Working-age CFR at 0.8% meets benchmark**: Adult case management appears adequate — the problem is at the extremes of age

### Treatment Center Capacity

| CTC | Beds | Occupied | Occupancy | IV Fluid (days) | ORS (days) | Chlorination | Status |
|-----|------|----------|-----------|-----------------|------------|-------------|--------|
| Gulu CTC | 80 | 72 | 90% | 3 | 5 | Yes | [WARNING] |
| Lira CTC | 40 | 38 | 95% | 1 | 2 | Yes | [CRITICAL] |
| Soroti CTC | 30 | 28 | 93% | 2 | 3 | No | [CRITICAL] |

- **[CRITICAL] Lira CTC: 1 day of IV fluid remaining at 95% occupancy**: A single surge day could exhaust supplies and force rationing of life-saving rehydration. Emergency resupply within 24 hours is non-negotiable
- **[CRITICAL] Soroti CTC lacks water chlorination**: A cholera treatment center without chlorinated water is itself a transmission risk — healthcare-associated cholera spread is documented
- **[WARNING] All three CTCs above 90% occupancy**: No surge capacity. If the declining trend reverses or a new geographic cluster emerges, there are functionally zero available beds

### WASH Context (Root Cause Layer)

| Subcounty | Safe Water Access | Latrine Coverage | Status |
|-----------|------------------|-----------------|--------|
| Soroti Town | 55% | 58% | [WARNING] |
| Gulu Central | 45% | 52% | [CRITICAL] |
| Lira East | 32% | 40% | [CRITICAL] |
| Gulu North | 28% | 35% | [CRITICAL] |

- **[CRITICAL] Gulu North: 28% safe water access, 35% latrine coverage**: The epidemiological conditions for sustained cholera transmission are fully present. Without WASH improvement, the outbreak will recur even if current cases are treated
- The correlation between WASH indicators and outbreak intensity is expected — cholera is fundamentally a WASH disease

### Cross-Dashboard Intelligence

- The epidemic curve decline (W50-W52) coincides with response deployment, but WASH indicators remain unchanged — the decline may reflect treatment/case management rather than transmission interruption
- Under-5 CFR links to pediatric care capacity — verify pediatric-specific rehydration protocols are being used (weight-based calculations differ from adults)
- Cholera disproportionately affects the malnourished — Karamoja/Northern region nutrition data (55% stunting) suggests overlapping vulnerability
- Oral cholera vaccine (OCV) should be considered for reactive vaccination in affected subcounties if available through ICG mechanism

### Recommendations

1. **EMERGENCY — Resupply Lira CTC within 24 hours**: IV Ringer's lactate and ORS must be airlifted/fast-tracked. At current consumption rate, stock-out is imminent. Contact UNICEF/WHO emergency supply pipeline
2. **EMERGENCY — Install chlorination at Soroti CTC**: Water chlorination at a CTC is a basic IPC standard. Procure chlorine solution and install dosing system immediately
3. **Immediate — Pediatric rehydration protocol review**: 4.8% under-5 CFR requires clinical audit. Deploy cholera clinical mentors with pediatric expertise to all CTCs. Ensure Plan C (IV rehydration) is initiated immediately for severely dehydrated children
4. **Short-term — Expand CTC surge capacity**: At 90-95% occupancy, pre-position additional tents/beds at each CTC. Consider community-based oral rehydration points (ORPs) to divert mild/moderate cases
5. **Short-term — WASH emergency response in Gulu North and Lira East**: Emergency water trucking, point-of-use water treatment distribution (chlorine tabs/Aquatabs), and emergency latrine construction
6. **Medium-term — Do NOT scale down prematurely**: Maintain full surveillance and response capacity for at least 4 weeks after the last confirmed case. Monitor for second wave

## K. pneumoniae Shows 48% Ceftriaxone Resistance — Empiric First-Line Treatment Failing for Nearly Half of Bloodstream Infections [CRITICAL]

### Resistance Heatmap Summary

| Organism | Ampicillin | Cipro | Ceftriaxone | Gentamicin | Meropenem | Other |
|----------|-----------|-------|-------------|------------|-----------|-------|
| E. coli | 82% [CRITICAL] | 45% [CRITICAL] | 32% [WARNING] | 28% [WARNING] | 3% [GOOD] | -- |
| K. pneumoniae | 95% [CRITICAL] | 52% [CRITICAL] | 48% [CRITICAL] | 38% [WARNING] | 8% [WARNING] | -- |
| S. aureus | -- | -- | -- | -- | -- | MRSA 35%, Vanc 0% [GOOD] |
| Salmonella | 68% [CRITICAL] | 18% [INFO] | 12% [INFO] | -- | -- | Chlor 55% [CRITICAL] |

### Critical Antimicrobial Resistance Findings

- **[CRITICAL] K. pneumoniae ceftriaxone resistance at 48%**: Ceftriaxone is the workhorse empiric antibiotic for suspected Gram-negative sepsis in Uganda. Nearly half of K. pneumoniae bloodstream infections are resistant — empiric treatment is failing for these patients before culture results return
- **[CRITICAL] E. coli ampicillin resistance at 82%**: Ampicillin should effectively be removed from empiric protocols for E. coli infections — only 18% susceptibility makes it clinically unreliable
- **[CRITICAL] K. pneumoniae 8% meropenem resistance**: Carbapenem resistance is the last-line red flag. While 8% seems low, any carbapenem resistance in resource-limited settings is alarming because there are no available alternatives
- **[WARNING] MRSA at 35%**: More than 1 in 3 S. aureus bloodstream infections is methicillin-resistant — oxacillin/cefazolin-based empiric therapy fails in these patients. Vancomycin susceptibility at 0% resistance is reassuring but vancomycin supply and monitoring capacity are often limited
- **[GOOD] Salmonella ciprofloxacin resistance at 18%**: Relatively preserved — fluoroquinolones remain viable for invasive Salmonella, but the 18% resistance should be monitored for upward trends

### Clinical Impact Assessment

- A patient presenting with Gram-negative sepsis treated empirically with ceftriaxone has a ~40% chance of receiving an ineffective antibiotic (weighted by organism prevalence)
- The clinical consequence: delayed effective therapy for bacteremia increases mortality 7-10% per hour of delay
- The resistance gradient (ampicillin > cipro > ceftriaxone > gentamicin > meropenem) follows global ESBL/resistance patterns — consistent with ESBL-producing Enterobacteriaceae driving multi-drug resistance

### AMR Drivers and Cross-Program Links

- **IPC**: Healthcare-associated transmission of resistant organisms (especially K. pneumoniae) requires hand hygiene, contact precautions, and environmental cleaning — IPC scores at lower facilities are critical
- **Antibiotic stewardship**: Over-the-counter antibiotic sales, empiric treatment without cultures, and incomplete courses all drive resistance. Community pharmacies and OPD prescribing patterns are key intervention points
- **Lab strengthening**: Blood culture and DST capacity determines whether resistance is detected — these data come from facilities with labs. Resistance at facilities without lab capacity is unknown and likely similar or worse
- **Neonatal sepsis**: K. pneumoniae is a leading cause of neonatal sepsis. 48% ceftriaxone resistance in newborn units is catastrophic — neonatal empiric protocols may need revision
- **Surgical prophylaxis**: Pre-operative antibiotic choices depend on local resistance patterns — surgical teams need these data

### Data Quality Considerations

- Blood culture data represent a selected sample — patients sick enough to have cultures drawn, at facilities with lab capacity. Community-acquired infections may have different resistance profiles
- Sample size per organism-antibiotic combination is not provided — small numbers can produce unstable percentages. Verify n>30 per cell before drawing conclusions
- Quality of AST (disk diffusion vs automated systems) affects accuracy — verify lab EQA participation
- Contamination rates should be reported — S. aureus in blood cultures can be contaminant (coagulase-negative) unless speciated accurately

### Recommendations

1. **Immediate — Revise empiric sepsis protocols**: Ceftriaxone monotherapy is inadequate for ~40-48% of Gram-negative BSIs. Consider ceftriaxone + gentamicin combination or carbapenem escalation protocols for severe sepsis, pending local guideline review with infectious disease team
2. **Immediate — Restrict ampicillin for Gram-negative infections**: At 82-95% resistance, ampicillin should be removed from empiric Gram-negative protocols. It retains a role for Enterococcal and Listeria infections only
3. **Short-term — Establish carbapenem stewardship**: The 3-8% meropenem resistance MUST NOT increase. Restrict carbapenems to confirmed ESBL infections with documented ceftriaxone resistance. Stewardship committee should approve each course
4. **Short-term — Neonatal unit AMR audit**: Cross-reference neonatal sepsis outcomes with these resistance data. If K. pneumoniae is causing neonatal deaths due to ceftriaxone resistance, empiric neonatal protocols need urgent revision
5. **Medium-term — Expand blood culture capacity**: These data are only available where cultures are performed. Expand blood culture capability to district hospitals to build a representative national AMR picture

## Schistosomiasis and STH MDA Coverage Below WHO 75% Threshold in Most Districts — Karamoja STH at 32% [CRITICAL]

| District | LF | Oncho | Schisto | STH | Trachoma | Status |
|----------|-------|-------|---------|-----|----------|--------|
| Lira | 78% [GOOD] | 82% [GOOD] | 65% [WARNING] | 72% [WARNING] | -- | Moderate |
| Gulu | 72% [WARNING] | 75% [GOOD] | 58% [CRITICAL] | 68% [WARNING] | -- | Weak |
| Soroti | 65% [WARNING] | -- | 48% [CRITICAL] | 55% [CRITICAL] | -- | Critical |
| Karamoja | -- | -- | -- | 32% [CRITICAL] | 42% [CRITICAL] | Emergency |

### Coverage vs WHO Targets

| Disease | WHO MDA Target | Best District | Worst District | Gap |
|---------|---------------|--------------|---------------|-----|
| Lymphatic filariasis | >65% | Lira (78%) | Soroti (65%) | Borderline |
| Onchocerciasis | >80% | Lira (82%) | Gulu (75%) | Near target |
| Schistosomiasis | >75% | Lira (65%) | Soroti (48%) | ALL below |
| STH | >75% | Lira (72%) | Karamoja (32%) | ALL below |
| Trachoma | >80% | Karamoja (42%) | -- | Far below |

- **[CRITICAL] No district meets the WHO 75% threshold for schistosomiasis MDA**: The best performer (Lira, 65%) is 10 points short. Schistosomiasis causes chronic anaemia, hepatic fibrosis, and bladder disease — suboptimal MDA allows continued transmission and morbidity
- **[CRITICAL] Karamoja STH at 32% coverage with only 45% geographic reach**: More than half of the geographic target area was not reached, and within reached areas, only 32% of the eligible population received treatment. This is program failure
- **[CRITICAL] Trachoma MDA at 42% in Karamoja**: Trachoma is the leading infectious cause of blindness. At 42% coverage, the community-level impact threshold cannot be achieved, and elimination targets are unattainable
- **[GOOD] LF and Oncho near or above targets in Lira**: These programs have benefited from longer community drug distributor (CDD) networks and partner support — a model for other NTDs

### Geographic Coverage Analysis

- Geographic coverage (proportion of target areas reached) is the first bottleneck — if the MDA team doesn't reach a subcounty, nobody in that subcounty gets treated
- Schistosomiasis geographic coverage ranges 60-80% — meaning 20-40% of endemic subcounties were not visited
- Karamoja geographic coverage for STH (45%) and trachoma (55%) reflects the access challenges of pastoralist and semi-nomadic populations

### Cross-Program Integration Opportunities

- **NTD-Nutrition**: STH (hookworm) causes iron-deficiency anaemia and contributes to stunting — Karamoja's 55% stunting rate is compounded by 32% STH MDA coverage. Deworming IS a nutrition intervention
- **NTD-WASH**: Schistosomiasis and STH are fundamentally WASH-related — MDA without WASH improvement leads to rapid reinfection. The same subcounties with low safe water access (28-55%) are the NTD-endemic zones
- **NTD-EPI integration**: MDA distribution uses community health workers similar to immunization outreach — integrated NTD/vitamin A/deworming campaigns during child health days can boost coverage
- **Trachoma-WASH-Eye health**: The SAFE strategy (Surgery, Antibiotics, Facial cleanliness, Environmental improvement) requires WASH investment alongside MDA. Antibiotic MDA alone is insufficient

### Data Quality Notes

- MDA coverage denominators are based on census projections — rapidly growing or displaced populations may have outdated denominators
- Coverage may be over-reported if drug distributors report to supervisors without verification — consider independent coverage surveys (lot quality assurance sampling)
- Geographic coverage percentages depend on how target areas are defined — administrative vs epidemiological mapping

### Recommendations

1. **Immediate — Karamoja MDA strategy redesign**: Fixed-point distribution fails for mobile populations. Implement mobile/outreach MDA teams following pastoralist movement patterns, market day distribution, and school-based deworming
2. **Short-term — Integrated NTD-child health days**: Combine STH deworming with vitamin A supplementation, growth monitoring, and immunization during quarterly child health days — proven to boost coverage
3. **Short-term — Schistosomiasis school-based MDA**: Praziquantel distribution through schools reaches school-age children (highest-risk group) efficiently. Target schools in endemic subcounties with >20% prevalence
4. **Medium-term — WASH-NTD co-investment**: Map NTD endemic areas against WASH coverage to identify co-priority zones. Every latrine built in an STH-endemic area is an NTD intervention
5. **Data action — Post-MDA coverage surveys**: Conduct LQAS in Soroti and Karamoja to validate reported MDA coverage — independent verification is essential for program planning

## IPC Scores Collapse Below HC IV Level — Apac HC III Has Zero Isolation and Surveillance Capacity [CRITICAL]

### IPC Domain Scores by Facility

| Domain | Mulago NRH | Gulu RRH | Soroti HC IV | Apac HC III | Minimum |
|--------|-----------|----------|-------------|-------------|----------|
| Hand hygiene | 78% [WARNING] | 58% [CRITICAL] | 42% [CRITICAL] | 28% [CRITICAL] | 80% |
| PPE availability | 82% [GOOD] | 62% [WARNING] | 48% [CRITICAL] | 32% [CRITICAL] | 80% |
| Waste management | 72% [WARNING] | 48% [CRITICAL] | 35% [CRITICAL] | 22% [CRITICAL] | 70% |
| Sterilization | 85% [GOOD] | 65% [WARNING] | 40% [CRITICAL] | 18% [CRITICAL] | 80% |
| Isolation capacity | 68% [WARNING] | 35% [CRITICAL] | 15% [CRITICAL] | 0% [CRITICAL] | 50% |
| Water supply | 90% [GOOD] | 52% [CRITICAL] | 38% [CRITICAL] | 25% [CRITICAL] | 80% |
| HAI surveillance | 55% [CRITICAL] | 22% [CRITICAL] | 8% [CRITICAL] | 0% [CRITICAL] | 50% |
| **Overall** | **75.7%** | **48.9%** | **32.3%** | **17.9%** | -- |

### Heatmap Pattern Analysis

- **[CRITICAL] Diagonal degradation from top-left to bottom-right**: Every IPC domain score decreases monotonically from Mulago to Apac — the IPC gradient perfectly mirrors facility level, with no exceptions
- **[CRITICAL] HAI surveillance is the weakest domain system-wide**: Even Mulago at only 55%. Gulu at 22%, Soroti at 8%, Apac at 0% — healthcare-associated infections are not being detected, measured, or acted upon at any level below national referral
- **[CRITICAL] Isolation capacity at 0% (Apac) and 15% (Soroti)**: These facilities cannot isolate an infectious patient — during a cholera, Ebola, or COVID outbreak, they become amplification points rather than containment facilities
- **[CRITICAL] Apac HC III sterilization at 18%**: Surgical instruments, delivery kits, and injection equipment are inadequately sterilized — this is a direct vector for HBV, HCV, HIV, and bacterial sepsis transmission

### Patient Safety Risk Stratification

- **Surgical site infection risk**: Apac HC III sterilization at 18% and Soroti at 40% — any procedure performed at these facilities carries elevated SSI risk
- **Neonatal sepsis risk**: Newborns at facilities with hand hygiene below 50% and sterilization below 50% face dramatically elevated healthcare-associated neonatal sepsis risk — linking directly to the neonatal care quality data
- **Maternal sepsis contribution**: 18% of maternal deaths are from sepsis — IPC scores at delivery facilities are a direct determinant
- **Outbreak amplification risk**: Facilities with isolation 0-15% and water supply 25-38% will amplify any outbreak that reaches them

### Cross-Program Impact

- **Surgical care**: Safe surgery requires sterilization >80%, hand hygiene >80%, and waste management >70%. No lower-level facility meets these. Safe surgery is impossible at Soroti HC IV and Apac HC III by IPC standards
- **Injection safety**: Low sterilization scores raise injection safety concerns for immunization, therapeutic injections, and blood draws
- **AMR**: Poor IPC drives healthcare-associated infection with resistant organisms — the AMR resistance data (48% ceftriaxone-resistant K. pneumoniae) is partly a consequence of poor IPC allowing nosocomial transmission of resistant strains
- **TB IPC**: Airborne infection control is a subset of IPC — facilities with overall IPC <50% almost certainly lack TB-specific measures (ventilation, N95 masks, cough etiquette)

### Recommendations

1. **Emergency — Water supply at Apac HC III (25%)**: A health facility without reliable water cannot maintain basic hygiene. Install water storage, repair or dig boreholes, and ensure 24/7 access
2. **Immediate — Hand hygiene stations at all facilities below 60%**: Alcohol-based hand rub at every patient contact point — this is the single most effective IPC intervention and relatively low-cost
3. **Immediate — Sterilization audit and equipment at Apac and Soroti**: Verify autoclave functionality, replace if broken, and train staff on standard sterilization protocols. Disposable equipment may bridge the gap
4. **Short-term — HAI surveillance establishment**: Even basic surveillance (tracking post-surgical infections and neonatal sepsis rates) would provide actionable data. Start with surgical site infection monitoring at Mulago and Gulu
5. **Medium-term — IPC committee activation at every facility**: WHO recommends a functional IPC committee with trained focal person. Budget for IPC supplies should be a protected line item in facility budgets
6. **Accountability — Link IPC scores to facility accreditation**: Facilities below 50% overall IPC should have a mandatory improvement plan with quarterly re-assessment

## Antibiotics Replace ORS+Zinc as Primary Diarrhoea Treatment in 4 Districts — WHO Protocol Inverted [CRITICAL]

| District | U5 Cases | ORS | Zinc | ORS+Zinc | IV Fluid | Antibiotics | Status |
|----------|---------|-----|------|----------|----------|------------|--------|
| Kampala | 8,200 | 82% | 68% | 62% | 12% | 35% | [WARNING] |
| Wakiso | 9,500 | 78% | 62% | 55% | 10% | 38% | [WARNING] |
| Lira | 5,900 | 68% | 42% | 35% | 9% | 48% | [CRITICAL] |
| Gulu | 6,800 | 65% | 38% | 32% | 8% | 52% | [CRITICAL] |
| Soroti | 5,200 | 58% | 32% | 25% | 6% | 58% | [CRITICAL] |
| Karamoja | 4,100 | 42% | 18% | 12% | 4% | 65% | [CRITICAL] |
| **WHO target** | -- | **>90%** | **>90%** | **>80%** | **As needed** | **<20%** | -- |

### Critical Treatment Quality Findings

- **[CRITICAL] Antibiotic use exceeds ORS+zinc in 4 districts**: In Gulu (52%), Soroti (58%), and Karamoja (65%), more children receive antibiotics than combined ORS+zinc. This is the exact opposite of WHO-recommended management — diarrhoea treatment is evidence-inverted
- **[CRITICAL] Karamoja ORS+zinc at only 12%**: 88% of children with diarrhoea do not receive the recommended treatment. Meanwhile 65% receive antibiotics, which are not indicated for most acute watery diarrhoea and drive antimicrobial resistance
- **[WARNING] Zinc systematically lags ORS by 20-24 points**: ORS is more available and familiar, but zinc is equally important for reducing duration and preventing recurrence. The gap suggests zinc stock-outs or provider unfamiliarity
- **[INFO] IV fluid rates (4-12%) suggest appropriate severity stratification**: IV fluids are reserved for severe dehydration (Plan C) — these rates are plausible and appropriate

### Antibiotic Overuse Analysis

- **[CRITICAL] Antibiotics should be used in <20% of acute diarrhoea cases** (only for dysentery/bloody diarrhoea or confirmed cholera). Rates of 35-65% indicate massive overuse
- Antibiotic overuse in childhood diarrhoea is the single largest driver of community AMR in low-resource settings — K. pneumoniae resistance data (48% ceftriaxone-resistant) is partly driven by this prescribing pattern
- The antibiotic-ORS relationship is inversely correlated (r ~ -0.95) — districts with less ORS use more antibiotics. Providers substitute what they have, not what evidence recommends

### Cross-Program Intelligence

- **Diarrhoea-WASH**: The 4,100 cases in Karamoja with 28% safe water access shows the WASH-diarrhoea causal chain. Treatment is downstream; prevention requires WASH investment
- **Diarrhoea-Nutrition**: Repeated diarrhoea drives malnutrition through nutrient malabsorption. The same districts with high diarrhoea burden (Karamoja, Soroti) show the worst nutrition indicators
- **Diarrhoea-Zinc-Growth**: Zinc supplementation during diarrhoea reduces episodes by 25% over 3 months — zinc IS a nutrition intervention delivered through the diarrhoea treatment platform
- **AMR link**: Unnecessary antibiotic prescribing for diarrhoea directly feeds the resistance crisis — every unnecessary course of amoxicillin or metronidazole for watery diarrhoea selects for resistant bacteria

### Data Quality Assessment

- Treatment percentages should sum approximately — ORS + no treatment + other should approximate 100%. ORS at 42% with antibiotics at 65% in Karamoja suggests overlap (children getting both) or data definition issues
- "ORS treatment" may count ORS given at facility but miss home ORS use — community-level ORS/zinc distribution through VHTs may not be captured
- Antibiotic rates may include prescriptions at pharmacies and private clinics, making the true rate even higher

### Recommendations

1. **Immediate — Emergency zinc supply to Karamoja and Soroti**: 18% and 32% zinc treatment indicates stock-outs. Procure co-packaged ORS+zinc kits to ensure both components are available together
2. **Immediate — Antibiotic prescribing audit**: Conduct clinical audit of diarrhoea prescriptions at high-antibiotic facilities. Distinguish appropriate use (dysentery) from inappropriate use (watery diarrhoea)
3. **Short-term — IMCI protocol reinforcement**: Retrain providers on diarrhoea assessment and treatment plan A/B/C. Emphasize: ORS+zinc first, antibiotics only for bloody diarrhoea
4. **Short-term — Community ORS+zinc distribution**: Train VHTs to dispense ORS+zinc at community level for uncomplicated diarrhoea — reduces facility burden and increases treatment rates
5. **Medium-term — Antibiotic stewardship at OPD level**: Implement prescription review, peer feedback, and formulary restrictions. The 65% antibiotic rate in Karamoja is both a child health problem and an AMR accelerator

| **WHO minimum** | **>2.0** | **<5 (target)** | **>80%** | **>80%** | -- |

### AFP Surveillance Analysis

- **[CRITICAL] Non-Polio AFP rate declined from 2.8 (Jan) to 0.7 (Dec) — a 75% collapse**: The WHO minimum standard is >2.0 per 100,000 children under 15. Rates below 1.0 for 4 consecutive months (Sep-Dec) indicates the surveillance system is no longer detecting AFP cases — NOT that paralysis isn't occurring
- **[CRITICAL] This means potential polio cases could circulate undetected**: AFP surveillance is the primary mechanism for polio detection. A rate below 1.0 is certification-level failure — the country cannot certify polio-free status with this surveillance quality
- **[WARNING] Stool adequacy declined from 78% to 58-60% during mid-year**: Stool adequacy (two adequate specimens within 14 days) below 80% means even detected AFP cases aren't being properly investigated
- **[INFO] AFP and stool adequacy show parallel decline**: Both dropped mid-year and partially recovered — suggesting a seasonal or workforce-related surveillance capacity issue

### Measles Surveillance and Outbreak

- **[CRITICAL] Measles incidence peaked at 62 per million in June**: 12x the elimination target (<5/million). This is an outbreak — the epidemic curve shows classic rise (Jan-Jun) and fall (Jun-Dec)
- **[WARNING] Lab confirmation dropped to 45% at outbreak peak**: When case volumes surged, lab capacity couldn't keep pace — exactly when confirmation matters most for characterizing outbreak epidemiology
- **[GOOD] Post-outbreak recovery**: Incidence returned to 10/million by December and lab confirmation recovered to 78% — suggesting outbreak response (likely reactive vaccination campaign) was effective
- **[WARNING] Measles lab confirmation at 78% (Dec) still below WHO 80% target**: Even at low case volumes, confirmation rates don't meet the standard

### Inverse Relationship: Measles Outbreak vs AFP Surveillance

- The measles outbreak surge (Jan-Jun) coincides exactly with AFP surveillance decline — surveillance officers likely redirected from routine AFP surveillance to measles outbreak response
- This is a critical system failure: outbreak response should not cannibalize routine surveillance. It suggests a single-officer dependency rather than a resilient system
- The post-outbreak AFP rate did NOT recover (0.7 in Dec vs 2.8 in Jan) — the surveillance workforce may be depleted, burned out, or reassigned permanently

### Cross-Program Links

- **Immunization-surveillance**: Measles outbreak at 62/million during months when DPT3 coverage was reported at 87% nationally — suggests immunity gaps in specific cohorts (missed children, zero-dose) rather than general coverage failure
- **Polio risk**: Declining AFP surveillance in a polio-risk country is an international health emergency concern. Global Polio Eradication Initiative partners should be alerted
- **Lab system**: Both AFP stool testing and measles confirmation rely on the same national reference laboratory — lab capacity is a shared bottleneck

### Data Quality Assessment

- The declining AFP rate is more likely to be a surveillance sensitivity issue than a true decline in paralytic illness — non-polio AFP incidence is biologically stable year-to-year
- Possible explanations for the decline: fewer surveillance officers, reduced active case search, clinician reporting fatigue, or changes in case definition application
- Measles case counts during outbreak peaks may include both confirmed and clinically compatible cases — the declining confirmation rate at peak reflects lab overload, not fewer true measles cases

### Recommendations

1. **EMERGENCY — AFP surveillance recovery plan**: The non-polio AFP rate below 1.0 for 4 months constitutes a polio program emergency. Deploy dedicated AFP surveillance officers, conduct active case searches at all hospitals and rehabilitation centers, and report to GPEI partners immediately
2. **Immediate — Zero-dose identification in measles-affected areas**: The outbreak revealed immunity gaps — map zero-dose and under-immunized children in outbreak-affected subcounties for targeted catch-up vaccination
3. **Short-term — Decouple routine surveillance from outbreak response**: Ensure dedicated surveillance officers maintain AFP and other routine surveillance even during outbreak surge. Surge staffing should be additional, not redirected
4. **Short-term — Lab capacity strengthening**: Target >80% stool adequacy and >80% measles confirmation. Ensure reference lab has sufficient reagents, staffing, and turnaround time
5. **Medium-term — Integrated surveillance strengthening**: AFP, measles, and other VPD surveillance share infrastructure. Invest in surveillance officers who monitor ALL VPDs rather than single-disease vertical systems

## Meningitis Alerts Surging 9x in 8 Weeks While Diarrhoea Subsides — Possible Meningitis Season Onset [CRITICAL]

### Alert Signal Trend (Stacked Area)

| Week | AWD | AFI | Meningitis | Measles | AFP | Total |
|------|-----|-----|-----------|---------|-----|-------|
| W44 | 8 | 12 | 2 | 3 | 1 | 26 |
| W45 | 12 | 15 | 1 | 2 | 1 | 31 |
| W46 | 18 | 14 | 3 | 2 | 0 | 37 |
| W47 | 25 | 18 | 4 | 1 | 1 | 49 |
| W48 | 32 | 22 | 5 | 1 | 0 | 60 |
| W49 | 28 | 25 | 8 | 0 | 0 | 61 |
| W50 | 22 | 20 | 12 | 0 | 0 | 54 |
| W51 | 15 | 18 | 15 | 0 | 0 | 48 |
| W52 | 12 | 16 | 18 | 0 | 0 | 46 |

### Three Distinct Surveillance Signals

**Signal 1: Meningitis Emerging [CRITICAL]**
- **[CRITICAL] Meningitis alerts rose from 2 (W44) to 18 (W52) — a 9x increase over 8 weeks**: This exponential growth pattern during the dry season (Nov-Dec) is consistent with the onset of meningococcal meningitis season in the African meningitis belt
- By W51, meningitis alerts surpassed AWD as the fastest-growing category — this is a sentinel signal that should trigger epidemic preparedness
- If the upward trajectory continues into January-March (peak meningitis season), epidemic threshold may be crossed within 2-4 weeks

**Signal 2: Diarrhoea Outbreak Subsiding [GOOD]**
- **[GOOD] AWD alerts peaked at 32 (W48) and declined to 12 (W52)**: Consistent with the cholera outbreak response data — treatment and WASH interventions appear to be controlling transmission
- The decline is steady (not oscillating) — suggesting genuine transmission reduction rather than reporting fatigue

**Signal 3: AFP Surveillance Failing [CRITICAL]**
- **[CRITICAL] Zero AFP alerts in W46, W48, W49, W50, W51, W52**: Six weeks of zero AFP signals in a population this size is biologically implausible and confirms the AFP surveillance collapse identified in the VPD data
- AFP disappearing from the IDSR alert system means the polio detection backbone is non-functional

### Stacked Area Interpretation

- The total alert volume shows a bulge (W47-W50) driven by simultaneous AWD and AFI surges, now subsiding
- But the composition is shifting: AWD and measles are declining while meningitis is rising — the area chart shows a clear category substitution pattern
- AFI (acute febrile illness) remains elevated (16-25/week) throughout — this is the background malaria/respiratory signal in endemic settings

### Cross-Category Intelligence

- The AWD decline and meningitis rise are seasonally coherent: wet season ends (diarrhoea drops), dry dusty season begins (meningitis rises)
- AFI persistence may mask other conditions — influenza, dengue, or other febrile illnesses could be hidden within the AFI category
- Zero measles alerts from W49 onward is consistent with post-outbreak immunity and/or outbreak response vaccination

### Recommendations

1. **EMERGENCY — Meningitis epidemic preparedness**: Pre-position CSF collection kits, ceftriaxone, and meningococcal vaccine at district hospitals in the Northern region. Alert the ICG (International Coordinating Group) for potential reactive vaccination
2. **Immediate — CSF sampling and lab confirmation**: Every meningitis alert should trigger lumbar puncture and rapid antigen testing to identify the serogroup (A, C, W, X) — this determines vaccine choice
3. **Immediate — Revive AFP surveillance**: Six consecutive weeks of zero AFP alerts is an emergency. Deploy rapid surveillance assessments at all reporting sites
4. **Short-term — IDSR timeliness and completeness audit**: Verify that all health facilities are reporting weekly. Silent facilities (no reports) should be investigated — zero alerts may reflect non-reporting rather than no cases
5. **Medium-term — Strengthen AFI differential diagnosis**: The persistent 16-25 weekly AFI alerts need better etiological characterization. Consider adding RDT-based categorization (malaria-positive vs malaria-negative febrile illness) to the IDSR framework

## Only 16.6% of Confirmed Hypertensives Achieve BP Control — 29,200 Uncontrolled Patients at Elevated CVD Risk [CRITICAL]

| Cascade Stage | Count | % of Screened | Stage Retention | Target |
|---------------|-------|--------------|-----------------|--------|
| Screened (40+) | 125,000 | 100% | -- | -- |
| Elevated BP | 42,000 | 33.6% | -- | Prevalence |
| Confirmed HTN | 35,000 | 28.0% | 83.3% of elevated | -- |
| Treatment initiated | 18,500 | 14.8% | 52.9% of confirmed | >80% |
| On treatment 6M | 11,200 | 9.0% | 60.5% of initiated | >80% |
| BP controlled | 5,800 | 4.6% | 51.8% of retained | >50% |

### Hypertension Cascade Failure Points

- **[CRITICAL] 16,500 confirmed hypertensives (47.1%) never initiated treatment**: The largest absolute gap is between diagnosis and treatment initiation. Nearly half of people told they have hypertension walk away without medication — barriers include: no NCD medicines in stock, provider not prescribing, patient not accepting diagnosis, cost of treatment
- **[CRITICAL] 7,300 patients (39.5%) dropped out within 6 months**: Treatment retention in chronic disease is the defining challenge. Unlike acute illness, hypertension requires lifelong daily medication with no immediate symptom relief — dropout is predictable without support systems
- **[WARNING] Only 51.8% of retained patients achieve BP control**: Even among those who stay on treatment, half are uncontrolled — suggesting suboptimal dosing, medication non-adherence, or inappropriate monotherapy
- **[INFO] Screening yield of 33.6% elevated BP is plausible**: Consistent with SSA hypertension prevalence estimates of 25-35% in adults over 40. Confirmation rate (83.3%) suggests reasonable re-measurement protocols

### Effective Coverage Calculation

- Effective coverage = controlled / confirmed = 5,800 / 35,000 = **16.6%**
- WHO HEARTS target for effective coverage is >50% by 2030 — current performance is 33 points below target
- Each percentage point of improvement in control represents ~350 patients with reduced stroke and heart attack risk

### Cross-Program Intelligence

- **HTN-Diabetes comorbidity**: Estimate 30-40% of hypertensives have concurrent diabetes — integrated NCD screening (BP + blood glucose) at the same visit doubles efficiency
- **HTN-HIV**: Aging PLHIV on ART have elevated cardiovascular risk. ART clinics should integrate BP screening — they already have the patient retention infrastructure that NCD clinics lack
- **HTN-Kidney disease**: Uncontrolled hypertension is the leading cause of chronic kidney disease in SSA. The 29,200 uncontrolled patients are silently developing renal damage
- **HTN-Maternal health**: Chronic hypertension in women of reproductive age increases pre-eclampsia risk — link NCD registers with ANC for flagging high-risk pregnancies

### Equity Considerations

- Screening population (40+) may exclude younger adults with hypertension — consider screening from age 30 in high-risk groups
- Urban vs rural treatment access likely drives the initiation gap — rural facilities may lack antihypertensives entirely
- Gender data not provided — women typically have higher screening rates but men have higher undiagnosed prevalence
- Cost of medication is a major barrier for chronic conditions — verify whether essential NCD medicines are on the free/subsidized list

### Data Quality Notes

- The cascade depends on linked patient records across screening, confirmation, initiation, and follow-up — if records are not linked, patients may be counted at wrong stages
- "Confirmed hypertensive" requires two elevated readings on separate occasions — verify whether the confirmation protocol is consistently applied
- "BP controlled" definition (<140/90) is the standard threshold — some guidelines now recommend <130/80 for high-risk patients

### Recommendations

1. **Immediate — Ensure antihypertensive availability at all HC III+**: Treatment cannot be initiated if medicines are not in stock. Verify amlodipine, enalapril/losartan, and hydrochlorothiazide availability at NCD service points
2. **Short-term — Simplified treatment protocols**: Implement WHO HEARTS protocol — standardized, simplified treatment algorithms reduce prescriber hesitation and improve consistency. Single-pill combinations improve adherence
3. **Short-term — Leverage ART retention model for NCD care**: HIV programs achieved >90% retention through community support, peer groups, and differentiated service delivery. Apply these same approaches to hypertension patients
4. **Medium-term — Community-based BP monitoring**: Train VHTs to measure BP and refill chronic prescriptions — decentralized care reduces the need for monthly facility visits and improves retention
5. **Data action — Establish NCD patient cohort tracking**: Move from aggregate counting to individual patient tracking (like the HIV cohort model) — this enables true cascade analysis and identifies exactly where each patient drops out

## Diabetes Complication Screening Near-Zero at Lower Facilities — Preventable Blindness, Amputation, and Kidney Failure Accumulating Silently [CRITICAL]

| Indicator | Mulago NRH | Gulu RRH | Lira HC IV | Target |
|-----------|-----------|----------|------------|--------|
| Patients enrolled | 2,800 | 850 | 320 | -- |
| HbA1c tested | 72% [WARNING] | 42% [CRITICAL] | 18% [CRITICAL] | >90% |
| HbA1c <7% (controlled) | 38% [CRITICAL] | 22% [CRITICAL] | 12% [CRITICAL] | >50% |
| Eye screening | 28% [CRITICAL] | 8% [CRITICAL] | 2% [CRITICAL] | Annual |
| Foot exam | 45% [WARNING] | 25% [CRITICAL] | 12% [CRITICAL] | Every visit |
| Renal function | 52% [WARNING] | 18% [CRITICAL] | 5% [CRITICAL] | Annual |
| Statin prescribed | 35% [CRITICAL] | 15% [CRITICAL] | 8% [CRITICAL] | >80% CVD risk |

### Glycemic Control Analysis

- **[CRITICAL] Only 38% of Mulago patients and 12% of Lira patients have HbA1c <7%**: The majority of diabetic patients at every facility are inadequately controlled. Chronic hyperglycemia drives microvascular complications (retinopathy, nephropathy, neuropathy) and macrovascular events (MI, stroke)
- **[CRITICAL] HbA1c testing itself is the first bottleneck**: At 18% in Lira, 82% of patients have no objective measure of glycemic control. Providers are managing diabetes blind — adjusting medications without knowing the result
- The control-to-testing ratio reveals treatment effectiveness: Mulago 38/72 = 53% of tested patients are controlled (reasonable), but Lira 12/18 = 67% (paradoxically higher — likely because only the best-managed patients get tested)

### Complication Prevention Gaps

- **[CRITICAL] Eye screening at 2% (Lira) and 8% (Gulu)**: Diabetic retinopathy is the leading cause of preventable blindness. At 2% screening, retinopathy will be detected only at the stage of irreversible vision loss
- **[CRITICAL] Renal function at 5% (Lira)**: Diabetic nephropathy progresses silently. Without annual creatinine/eGFR, patients reach end-stage renal disease without any warning or intervention opportunity
- **[WARNING] Foot exam at 12-45%**: Diabetic foot disease leading to amputation is one of the most feared and costly complications. A simple annual foot exam with monofilament can identify at-risk feet before ulceration
- **[CRITICAL] Statin prescribing at 8-35%**: Most diabetic patients over 40 should receive statins for cardiovascular risk reduction. The 8% rate at Lira means cardiovascular prevention is essentially absent from diabetes care

### Cross-Program Links

- **Diabetes-HTN**: 60-70% of diabetics have concurrent hypertension — verify whether BP is being measured at diabetes visits (the hypertension cascade data suggest it is, but BP control rates are similarly poor)
- **Diabetes-HIV**: Antiretroviral therapy (particularly PIs and some NRTIs) can cause metabolic syndrome — PLHIV on ART need metabolic screening, linking HIV and NCD programs
- **Diabetes-Eye health**: The 2-8% eye screening rate identifies a critical gap in the eye health program. Training primary care providers in basic fundoscopy or deploying mobile retinal cameras would transform screening coverage
- **Diabetes-Kidney disease**: The 5% renal function testing rate means diabetic kidney disease is invisible in the data — many patients presenting with "unexplained" renal failure may have undiagnosed diabetic nephropathy
- **Diabetes-Pregnancy**: Gestational diabetes and pre-existing diabetes in pregnancy carry major risks — link diabetes registers with ANC registers

### Data Quality Assessment

- "Patients enrolled" likely undercounts true diabetic population — facility-based registers capture only those who present. Population-based prevalence is likely 2-5x the enrolled count
- HbA1c testing requires specific lab equipment not available at lower facilities — point-of-care HbA1c devices could transform access
- Complication screening rates may be inflated if a single annual screening counts toward quarterly reporting

### Recommendations

1. **Immediate — Deploy point-of-care HbA1c at district hospitals**: A $2,000 device transforms diabetes management. Lira HC IV at 18% HbA1c testing is not a resource allocation problem — it's a technology deployment gap
2. **Immediate — Statin initiation protocol**: Implement a standing order that every diabetic patient over 40 receives a statin unless contraindicated. The 8% prescribing rate is clinical inertia, not evidence-based restraint
3. **Short-term — Annual complication screening package**: Create a bundled annual screen (HbA1c + creatinine + lipids + foot exam + eye check) to be completed at a single visit. Bundling improves completion rates
4. **Short-term — Task-shift eye screening**: Train diabetes clinic nurses in direct ophthalmoscopy or deploy phone-based retinal imaging. Referral to ophthalmologist only for detected pathology
5. **Medium-term — Differentiated NCD service delivery**: Apply the HIV differentiated model — stable patients with controlled HbA1c get 3-6 month medication refills and reduced visits. Unstable patients get intensified management. This frees clinic capacity for the 62-88% who need active intervention

## Only 8% of Eligible Women Screened This Year — 3,300 Precancerous Lesions Detected But Untreated [CRITICAL]

| Cascade Stage | Count | % of Eligible | Stage Retention |
|---------------|-------|--------------|------------------|
| Eligible (25-49) | 850,000 | 100% | -- |
| Ever screened | 145,000 | 17.1% | -- |
| Screened this year | 68,000 | 8.0% | -- |
| VIA/HPV positive | 5,400 | 0.6% | 7.9% positivity |
| Treated (cryo/LEEP) | 2,100 | -- | 38.9% of positive |
| Cancer referred | 320 | -- | 5.9% of positive |
| Cancer Tx initiated | 185 | -- | 57.8% of referred |

### Screening Coverage Crisis

- **[CRITICAL] 8% annual screening rate**: At this pace, screening the eligible population once would take 12.5 years — far beyond the WHO target of 70% of women screened by age 35 and again by age 45 with a high-performance test
- **[CRITICAL] Lifetime screening of only 17.1%**: 83% of women 25-49 have NEVER been screened. Cervical cancer is the leading cause of cancer death in Ugandan women, and it is almost entirely preventable with screening
- **[INFO] 7.9% positivity rate is within expected range**: For VIA screening in SSA, positivity of 5-15% is typical. This suggests screening quality is reasonable — the problem is volume, not accuracy

### Screen-and-Treat Gap

- **[CRITICAL] Only 38.9% of screen-positive women received treatment**: 3,300 women were told they have precancerous lesions and then received no cryotherapy or LEEP. This is the most devastating gap — detection without treatment gives no survival benefit and causes psychological harm
- **[WARNING] The treatment gap is likely driven by**: Cryotherapy equipment unavailability at screening sites (requires a screen-and-treat same-day model), referral loss between screening and treatment facilities, LEEP capacity concentrated at referral hospitals only
- **[CRITICAL] Only 57.8% of cancer referrals initiate treatment**: 135 women with confirmed cervical cancer were referred but never started treatment — these women will likely die from a treatable cancer

### Equity Dimensions

- Rural women face the greatest screening barriers — if screening sites are concentrated in urban centers, the 8% rate hides near-zero rural coverage
- HIV-positive women have 6x the cervical cancer risk and should be screened annually — verify whether ART clinics offer integrated cervical cancer screening
- Socioeconomic status determines whether women can travel to referral hospitals for LEEP or cancer treatment — the 42% referral-to-treatment loss is partly an access/cost barrier

### Cross-Program Links

- **HIV-Cervical cancer**: WHO recommends cervical cancer screening integrated into HIV care. ART clinics see women regularly — this is the most efficient screening platform for the highest-risk group
- **HPV vaccination**: The screening cascade addresses the current burden, but HPV vaccination of adolescent girls (9-14) prevents future burden. Link to EPI data on HPV vaccine coverage
- **Palliative care**: The 135 women with cancer who did not start treatment need palliative care — link to palliative services data
- **Reproductive health**: Screen-positive women need counseling on continued FP and reproductive planning

### Data Quality Notes

- Eligible population (850,000) is based on census projections — verify denominator currency
- "Ever screened" may overcount if women screened at multiple facilities are counted multiple times
- VIA positivity can vary with provider training quality — VIA has higher inter-observer variability than HPV testing
- Cancer referral-to-treatment gap may reflect data linkage failure rather than true loss — referral hospitals may have treated patients without feedback to the screening facility

### Recommendations

1. **Immediate — Screen-and-treat in a single visit**: Every screening site must have functional cryotherapy or thermal ablation available. The 61% treatment gap is unacceptable — if a woman tests positive, she must be treated the same day
2. **Immediate — Integrate screening into ART clinics**: HIV-positive women are highest priority. ART clinics already have the infrastructure, appointment systems, and patient retention needed for screening programs
3. **Short-term — Transition to HPV testing**: Self-collected HPV testing (molecular) is more sensitive than VIA, reduces provider dependency, and enables community-based screening. WHO now recommends HPV DNA testing as the primary screening method
4. **Short-term — Cancer treatment navigation**: Assign patient navigators to every woman referred for cancer treatment. The 42% referral loss represents women falling through system cracks
5. **Medium-term — HPV vaccination catch-up**: If HPV vaccine coverage in adolescent girls is low, consider a multi-age cohort catch-up campaign to cover girls up to 18-25 — this is the most cost-effective cervical cancer intervention

## Mental Health Treatment Gap Exceeds 90% Nationally — Karamoja Has Zero Dedicated Mental Health Staff [CRITICAL]

| District | MH OPD Visits | Depression Screen | Epilepsy Tx | AUD ID'd | Med Avail | Staff/100K |
|----------|--------------|------------------|-------------|----------|-----------|------------|
| Kampala | 4,200 | 15% | 680 | 320 | 72% | 1.8 |
| Wakiso | 2,800 | 10% | 420 | 180 | 58% | 0.8 |
| Gulu | 1,200 | 5% | 280 | 95 | 35% | 0.3 |
| Lira | 950 | 4% | 220 | 72 | 32% | 0.2 |
| Soroti | 620 | 3% | 150 | 45 | 28% | 0.1 |
| Karamoja | 180 | 1% | 42 | 15 | 12% | 0.0 |
| **WHO minimum** | -- | -- | -- | -- | **100%** | **>2.0** |

### Mental Health Treatment Gap Estimation

- **[CRITICAL] Depression screening at 1-15%**: WHO estimates depression prevalence at 5-8% of adults. Even Kampala screens only 15% — meaning 85-99% of depression cases go undetected and untreated
- **[CRITICAL] Karamoja: 0.0 mental health staff per 100,000**: A district with high trauma exposure (conflict, displacement, food insecurity) has literally zero dedicated mental health professionals. All mental health services depend on task-shifted general health workers, if any
- **[CRITICAL] Psychotropic medication availability at 12% in Karamoja**: Essential medications (phenobarbital for epilepsy, amitriptyline for depression, chlorpromazine for psychosis, diazepam for acute episodes) are absent 88% of the time — people with treatable conditions suffer without medication
- **[WARNING] Alcohol use disorder identification is extremely low**: 15-320 cases identified across districts where prevalence estimates suggest thousands affected. Harmful alcohol use drives injury, GBV, liver disease, and mental health crises — and is almost invisible in the data

### Epilepsy Treatment Gap

- **[CRITICAL] Estimated epilepsy prevalence is ~1% in SSA**: For Kampala (~2M population), expected cases would be ~20,000 — with 680 on treatment, the treatment gap is ~97%. This is one of the most treatable neurological conditions (phenobarbital costs $5/year)
- The treatment gap is even wider in rural districts where traditional beliefs about epilepsy delay or prevent biomedical care-seeking
- Children with untreated epilepsy cannot attend school — linking mental/neurological health to educational outcomes

### Cross-Program Intelligence

- **Mental health-HIV**: Depression is the strongest predictor of ART non-adherence. With 85-99% of depression undetected, a significant proportion of ART defaulters may have untreated depression
- **Mental health-TB**: TB treatment adherence similarly affected by depression and substance use — the 28% LTFU in Karamoja TB data may partly reflect untreated mental health conditions
- **Mental health-Maternal**: Perinatal depression affects 15-25% of women in SSA — with 1-15% screening rates, maternal mental health is essentially invisible. Untreated maternal depression affects bonding, breastfeeding, and child development
- **Alcohol-Injury**: Alcohol use disorder drives road traffic injuries, interpersonal violence, and unintentional injuries — the injury data should be cross-referenced with AUD identification
- **Mental health-NCD**: Depression worsens outcomes for diabetes, hypertension, and cancer — integrated NCD-mental health screening is WHO-recommended
- **Conflict-mental health**: Northern districts (Gulu, Lira) have high PTSD prevalence from the LRA conflict era — the low service availability does not match the elevated need

### Equity Analysis

- The 23x gap in OPD visits (Kampala 4,200 vs Karamoja 180) reflects the most extreme health equity gap in any program area. Mental health is the most neglected health program
- Staff concentration in Kampala (1.8/100K, still below WHO minimum of 2.0) means the entire country is understaffed, but rural areas have essentially nothing
- Stigma is an additional barrier that data cannot capture — many people with mental health conditions do not seek care even where services exist

### Data Quality Flags

- Mental health data is the least complete in most HMIS systems. These numbers likely represent significant underreporting
- "Depression screened" may count only those screened with a validated tool (PHQ-9) — informal clinical impressions may not be recorded
- Alcohol use disorder identification depends entirely on provider willingness to screen and document — stigma affects recording

### Recommendations

1. **Immediate — Essential psychotropic medicines on essential medicines list**: Phenobarbital, amitriptyline, chlorpromazine, haloperidol, and diazepam must be continuously available at HC III+ level. These are among the most cost-effective medicines in the WHO essential list
2. **Immediate — Integrate depression screening into ANC and ART**: PHQ-2 screening (2 questions, 30 seconds) at every ANC visit and ART refill visit. Positive screens get PHQ-9 full assessment. This piggybacks on existing high-volume platforms
3. **Short-term — mhGAP training for general health workers**: WHO's Mental Health Gap Action Programme trains non-specialist health workers to manage depression, psychosis, epilepsy, and substance use. Deploy to districts with <0.5 MH staff per 100K
4. **Short-term — Epilepsy treatment scale-up**: At $5/year for phenobarbital, treating epilepsy is among the most cost-effective interventions in medicine. Target schools and communities for case identification and referral
5. **Medium-term — Community mental health worker cadre**: Train and deploy community mental health workers (equivalent to VHTs for mental health) for psychosocial support, medication adherence, and stigma reduction

| Raised glucose | 8.2% | Halt rise | [WARNING] |
